usageContext: surprise or mild frustration, safe in any company including kids and elders. '¡Híjole!' and '¡chin!' are the same tier — the polite way to react.

examples.text: ¡Caray, otra vez se fue la luz!
examples.translation: Geez, the power went out again!

usageContext: the quintessential Mexican reaction of surprise, concern, or 'that's rough' — completely clean. '¡Híjole, qué pena!'
'¡chin!' is the mild, socially-safe clip of the vulgar 'chingada' — like 'shoot' for a stronger word. Reaction to a small mishap.

natural: no f***ing way! / you gotta be kidding me!
usageContext: vulgar but ubiquitous expression of disbelief, shock, or 'come on!'. Stronger than '¡no manches!' (its clean twin). Among close friends it barely registers; in mixed/formal company it's crude.
warningNotes: Understand it everywhere; use '¡no manches!' instead until you know the room. Never with elders, kids, or at work.

examples.translation: They fired you? You're kidding me!

usageContext: an all-purpose derogatory intensifier before a noun: 'pinche tráfico' (damn traffic), 'pinche güey' (that damn guy), 'pinche frío'. Adds contempt or just emphasis depending on tone.
warningNotes: Common but genuinely vulgar and often insulting when aimed at a person ('pinche + [person]'). Recognize it; deploy with care and only among friends.

examples.translation: Damn traffic, I've been at it an hour!

whoUses: everyone informally, in anger or teasing
usageContext: 'qué pendejo' (what an idiot), 'no seas pendejo' (don't be dumb), 'hacerse pendejo' (to play dumb / slack off). Between close friends it can be near-affectionate ribbing; at a stranger it's fighting words.
warningNotes: Severity swings hard on tone and relationship. As a learner you can't reliably land the affectionate version — recognize it; use the mild 'menso/tonto' instead.

examples.text: No seas pendejo, ¡te está tomando el pelo!
examples.translation: Don't be an idiot, he's pulling your leg!

usageContext: remarkably two-faced: an insult ('qué cabrón, me engañó'), a compliment ('está bien cabrón' = it's badass / he's a beast), and buddy address ('¿qué onda, cabrón?'). 'Está cabrón' = it's tough/hardcore. Tone is everything.
warningNotes: One of the hardest words to use correctly — the same word praises or insults depending on tone and bond. Recognize all senses; produce none until fluent.

whoUses: pervasive in Mexican Spanish, across a huge range of forms
usageContext: the root of a vast, uniquely Mexican family: 'chingón' (badass/awesome), 'chingadera' (crappy thing/dirty trick), 'no chingues' (knock it off), 'vete a la chingada' (get lost — strong), 'ya nos chingaron' (we're screwed), 'chinga tu madre' (the gravest insult, severity 7). Octavio Paz devoted an essay to this verb's centrality.
warningNotes: Enormous severity range — 'chingón' is almost playful, 'chinga tu madre' can start a fight. The whole family is best understood, not produced, by a learner; 'fregar' gives you a clean substitute for most senses.

examples.text: Ese taquero hace unos tacos bien chingones.
examples.translation: That taco guy makes some seriously badass tacos.

natural: the 'madre' family: from 'a toda madre' (awesome) to grave insults
whoUses: pervasive; the meaning flips wildly with the phrase
usageContext: 'madre' anchors dozens of expressions across the whole polarity: 'a toda madre' = awesome, 'qué padre' vs 'qué madres' = what the hell, 'me vale madres' = I don't give a damn, 'un desmadre' = chaos/wild party, 'partir la madre' = to beat up, 'no tiene madre' = it's shameless. Insulting someone's actual 'madre' is the deepest offense in Mexican culture.
saferAlternatives: a toda madre → 'padrísimo'; me vale madres → 'me da igual'
warningNotes: The single most culturally loaded word in Mexican profanity. Positive and obscene senses share the word; misjudging lands badly. Recognize the common set; avoid producing the insults entirely.

examples.text: La fiesta estuvo a toda madre.
